content.text: is the set of policies, mechanisms, and automated processes that govern how long an AI agent system keeps each piece of data it produces, how that data is stored over time, and how it is deleted or destroyed when the retention period expires. It answers three questions for every record: how long do we keep it, where do we keep it as time passes, and how do we prove we deleted it.

In agentic AI systems, retention is more complex than in conventional applications because agents produce many distinct data categories simultaneously: user conversation transcripts, tool call inputs and outputs, memory snapshots, intermediate reasoning traces, task artifacts, and audit events. Each category may carry different legal, regulatory, or business requirements around how long it must — or must not — exist.

Agentic systems accumulate data at a rate proportional to the autonomy and throughput of the agents. A production agent handling hundreds of tasks per day can write gigabytes of logs, memory entries, and tool outputs within weeks. Without explicit retention governance, three failure modes become likely.

GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form that identifies individuals no longer than necessary. CCPA creates similar obligations for US consumers. An agent that stores user conversation transcripts indefinitely, or that keeps PII embedded in tool outputs, can breach these obligations even if the data was collected lawfully in the first place.
Retaining every agent trace forever is unnecessary and expensive. Cold-tier storage costs orders of magnitude less than hot-tier, but only if data is actually moved there.
Data that has been deleted cannot be exfiltrated. Retaining user sessions or credentials beyond their useful life extends the window during which a breach is catastrophic.
Defined retention schedules with audit trails satisfy regulators and reduce the scope of discovery in litigation.
Automated tiering (hot → warm → cold → purge) aligns storage cost with data value over time.
Clear data lineage and tagging allow the system to locate and delete all records tied to a specific user identity on demand.
Expired data that is genuinely deleted cannot be leaked. Crypto-shredding makes deletion instantaneous even for petabyte-scale datasets.
Immutable deletion logs prove to regulators and auditors that obligations were met without relying on manual records.
Retention requires classification at write time, a policy engine, scheduled jobs, and cross-system coordination. Each is a potential point of failure.
Deleted data must also be removed from backups, or the backup must be excluded from retention scope with a documented justification.
Deleting a raw record does not automatically delete embeddings, aggregates, or fine-tuning datasets derived from it. Lineage tracking must extend to derived artifacts.
Conversation history loaded into a model’s context window is ephemeral, but if it is logged, that log is subject to retention. Logging granularity must be a deliberate decision.
Soft deletes (marking a record as deleted) satisfy many application needs but do not satisfy GDPR erasure obligations unless the data is also purged from backups.
Retention operates in four layers that work together:

Every record written by the agent system receives a label: data category (conversation, tool call, memory, audit), sensitivity level (public, internal, confidential, PII-bearing), and originating user identity. Labels are written as metadata alongside the record.

A policy database maps (data category, sensitivity) pairs to retention durations and storage tier schedules. The engine also holds jurisdiction-specific rules (e.g., EU users require deletion within X days of account closure).

A background daemon runs on a schedule and evaluates each record’s age against its policy. Records are promoted from hot (fast, expensive) to warm (object store) to cold (archive), and ultimately scheduled for deletion.

Deletion is either hard delete (remove the bytes) or crypto-shredding (delete the encryption key, making the ciphertext permanently unreadable). Either action is recorded in an append-only audit log with a timestamp, the acting entity, and a record identifier. A verification job confirms the audit log entry matches the absence of the data.

attrs.alt: Data Retention Architecture: Data sources classified and tagged, routed through policy engine, stored across hot/warm/cold/purge tiers with encryption and access controls

content.text: The diagram shows the four-stage pipeline. Data sources (conversations, tool call logs, memory snapshots, task outputs, audit events) are written to the ingestion layer, which attaches classification labels. The policy engine evaluates each labeled record against jurisdiction-aware retention schedules and deletion triggers. Data moves through storage tiers as it ages, with all operations recorded in an immutable audit log.
Your agent handles any user-identifiable data (names, emails, session IDs, IP addresses embedded in requests).
You operate in a regulated industry (finance, healthcare, legal, EU/UK market) where retention periods are mandated externally.
Agent throughput is high enough that storage costs will compound materially without tiering.
You need to support right-to-erasure or data export requests from users or regulators.
Your security model requires minimizing the window in which sensitive data exists.
You do not need an elaborate retention system for ephemeral research agents or prototypes that never touch real user data — but you should make that scope explicit rather than leaving it implicit.

List every type of record the agent system writes: conversation messages, tool call payloads, tool results, memory store entries, reasoning traces, task outputs, and system audit events. For each, note what PII it may contain and the business or legal justification for keeping it.

Assign each category a retention duration. Typical ranges: session transcripts 30–90 days (or until user deletes account), tool call logs 1 year (debugging, billing), audit events 7 years (regulatory), derived embeddings match source record retention.
Extend the write path to attach
to each record. Do this at the schema level, not as an afterthought.
Build or adopt a policy engine.
The engine resolves (category, jurisdiction, sensitivity) to a retention rule. Keep policies in a versioned config file or policy-as-code system so changes are auditable.
A scheduled job queries records where
and either deletes them or promotes them to the next tier. Run this job at least daily.
Build a user-scoped deletion API: given a
, find all records with that ID across all tiers and delete them immediately, regardless of their
. Write an audit entry for each deletion.
After each deletion run, sample a fraction of deleted record IDs and confirm they are absent from all tiers. Log the verification result alongside the deletion audit entries.

Confirm that deleted records are also excluded from backup restoration paths. Either exclude the relevant tables from backup scope or establish a backup retention policy shorter than the primary retention policy.
Tagging agent records at write time

import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class DataCategory(str, Enum):
    CONVERSATION = "conversation"
    TOOL_CALL = "tool_call"
    MEMORY = "memory"
    TASK_OUTPUT = "task_output"
    AUDIT = "audit"

class Sensitivity(str, Enum):
    PUBLIC = "public"
    INTERNAL = "internal"
    CONFIDENTIAL = "confidential"
    PII = "pii"

# Retention policy: (category, sensitivity) -> days
RETENTION_DAYS: dict[tuple[DataCategory, Sensitivity], int] = {
    (DataCategory.CONVERSATION, Sensitivity.PII): 30,
    (DataCategory.CONVERSATION, Sensitivity.INTERNAL): 90,
    (DataCategory.TOOL_CALL, Sensitivity.INTERNAL): 365,
    (DataCategory.TOOL_CALL, Sensitivity.CONFIDENTIAL): 365,
    (DataCategory.MEMORY, Sensitivity.PII): 30,
    (DataCategory.MEMORY, Sensitivity.INTERNAL): 180,
    (DataCategory.TASK_OUTPUT, Sensitivity.INTERNAL): 90,
    (DataCategory.AUDIT, Sensitivity.INTERNAL): 365 * 7,  # 7 years
}

@dataclass
class AgentRecord:
    record_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    data_category: DataCategory = DataCategory.TOOL_CALL
    sensitivity: Sensitivity = Sensitivity.INTERNAL
    user_id: Optional[str] = None
    payload: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc))
    expires_at: datetime = field(init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        days = RETENTION_DAYS.get(
            (self.data_category, self.sensitivity),
            365,  # default: 1 year
        )
        self.expires_at = self.created_at + timedelta(days=days)

def write_agent_record(db, category: DataCategory, sensitivity: Sensitivity,
                       user_id: str, payload: dict) -> AgentRecord:
    record = AgentRecord(
        data_category=category,
        sensitivity=sensitivity,
        user_id=user_id,
        payload=payload,
    )
    db.insert("agent_records", {
        "record_id": record.record_id,
        "data_category": record.data_category.value,
        "sensitivity": record.sensitivity.value,
        "user_id": record.user_id,
        "payload": record.payload,
        "created_at": record.created_at.isoformat(),
        "expires_at": record.expires_at.isoformat(),
    })
    return record

import asyncio
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

async def run_deletion_job(db, audit_log) -> dict:
    """Delete all records past their expires_at and return a summary."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    # Find expired records across all categories
    expired = await db.query(
        "SELECT record_id, data_category, user_id, expires_at "
        "FROM agent_records WHERE expires_at < $1",
        now,
    )

    deleted_count = 0
    errors = []

    for row in expired:
        try:
            await db.execute(
                "DELETE FROM agent_records WHERE record_id = $1",
                row["record_id"],
            )
            # Write an immutable audit entry
            await audit_log.append({
                "event": "record_deleted",
                "record_id": row["record_id"],
                "data_category": row["data_category"],
                "user_id": row["user_id"],
                "expired_at": row["expires_at"].isoformat(),
                "deleted_at": now.isoformat(),
                "triggered_by": "ttl_daemon",
            })
            deleted_count += 1
        except Exception as exc:
            logger.error("Failed to delete %s: %s", row["record_id"], exc)
            errors.append({"record_id": row["record_id"], "error": str(exc)})

    summary = {
        "run_at": now.isoformat(),
        "deleted": deleted_count,
        "errors": len(errors),
    }
    logger.info("Deletion job complete: %s", summary)
    return summary

# Schedule: call from a cron job or Temporal workflow
# asyncio.run(run_deletion_job(db, audit_log))

from datetime import datetime, timezone

async def erase_user_data(db, audit_log, user_id: str) -> dict:
    """
    Immediately delete all records for a user across all tables.
    This satisfies GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure requests.
    ""\"
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    tables = [
        "agent_records",
        "memory_entries",
        "conversation_messages",
        "task_outputs",
    ]
    total_deleted = 0

    for table in tables:
        result = await db.execute(
            f"DELETE FROM {table} WHERE user_id = $1 RETURNING record_id",
            user_id,
        )
        record_ids = [r["record_id"] for r in result]
        total_deleted += len(record_ids)

        for rid in record_ids:
            await audit_log.append({
                "event": "user_erasure",
                "table": table,
                "record_id": rid,
                "user_id": user_id,
                "erased_at": now.isoformat(),
                "triggered_by": "user_request",
            })

    # Also revoke any crypto keys tied to this user (crypto-shredding)
    await revoke_user_encryption_keys(user_id)
    await audit_log.append({
        "event": "encryption_keys_revoked",
        "user_id": user_id,
        "revoked_at": now.isoformat(),
    })

    return {"user_id": user_id, "total_records_erased": total_deleted}

async def revoke_user_encryption_keys(user_id: str):
    # Implementation depends on your KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, HashiCorp Vault)
    # e.g.: kms_client.schedule_key_deletion(KeyId=f"alias/user-{user_id}")
    pass

An enterprise agentic assistant handles employee HR queries. Each session may contain salary information (confidential), health-related questions (sensitive category under GDPR), and audit-mandatory actions like approvals. The system must comply with EU GDPR (retain personal data only as long as necessary) and local HR record-keeping laws (certain employment records must be kept for 6 years).

The retention setup: conversation transcripts tagged PII expire after 30 days; tool call logs (used for billing reconciliation) expire after 1 year; approval audit records expire after 7 years. When an employee leaves the company, a right-to-erasure request fires immediately, deleting conversation and memory records regardless of scheduled expiry, while preserving audit records under the legal hold exception.

from enum import Enum
from datetime import timedelta

class HRDataCategory(str, Enum):
    CONVERSATION = "conversation"        # expires 30 days
    TOOL_CALL = "tool_call"              # expires 1 year
    APPROVAL_AUDIT = "approval_audit"    # expires 7 years (legal hold)
    HEALTH_QUERY = "health_query"        # expires 30 days (GDPR sensitive cat.)

# Retention schedule for HR assistant
HR_RETENTION: dict[HRDataCategory, timedelta] = {
    HRDataCategory.CONVERSATION: timedelta(days=30),
    HRDataCategory.TOOL_CALL: timedelta(days=365),
    HRDataCategory.APPROVAL_AUDIT: timedelta(days=365 * 7),
    HRDataCategory.HEALTH_QUERY: timedelta(days=30),
}

# Legal hold categories exempt from user-initiated erasure
LEGAL_HOLD_CATEGORIES = {HRDataCategory.APPROVAL_AUDIT}

async def erase_employee_on_offboarding(db, audit_log, employee_id: str):
    """Delete non-legal-hold records when an employee leaves."""
    erasable = [
        c.value for c in HRDataCategory
        if c not in LEGAL_HOLD_CATEGORIES
    ]
    for category in erasable:
        await db.execute(
            "DELETE FROM hr_agent_records "
            "WHERE user_id = $1 AND data_category = $2",
            employee_id, category,
        )
        await audit_log.append({
            "event": "offboarding_erasure",
            "user_id": employee_id,
            "category": category,
        })
    # Approval audit records remain until their 7-year TTL fires
Treating all data as one category.
A single retention period for all agent data conflates records with very different legal requirements. Tag at the category level from day one.
Deleting a conversation record does not delete the vector embedding generated from it. Track derived artifacts and include them in deletion pipelines.
flag satisfies application reads but leaves bytes on disk. GDPR erasure requires the bytes to be gone (or the encryption key to be revoked).

Records deleted from the primary database reappear when the backup is restored. Either establish a backup retention policy shorter than the primary, or exclude the table from backup scope and document the justification.
Storing user IDs in opaque fields.
is embedded in a JSON blob rather than a top-level column, the right-to-erasure query cannot find it efficiently. Store user identity as an indexed column.

Writing an audit log entry for a deletion that silently failed (due to a constraint error or transaction rollback) gives a false compliance signal. Always verify the delete executed and record the verification result.
Application logs (not agent records) frequently capture request payloads containing PII. Apply the same retention policy to log streams as to structured records.
Tag at the point of creation.
Retrofit classification is error-prone. Attach category, sensitivity, and user identity metadata in the same transaction as the data write.
Deleting millions of individual objects from S3 or GCS is slow and expensive. Encrypt each user’s data under a user-specific key in KMS; deletion becomes a single key revocation.
Audit records should be written to a store that does not support in-place updates or deletes (e.g., a write-once S3 bucket with object lock, or an append-only Postgres table guarded by a trigger).
Store retention periods in configuration, not hard-coded constants. Regulatory requirements change; updating a config file and deploying is far safer than patching code.
Align backup TTL with primary TTL.
If primary retention is 30 days, set backup retention to 30 days or shorter. Most cloud backup services support configurable retention windows.
The job should be safe to run multiple times without double-counting or spurious audit entries. Use
Write integration tests that insert records for a user, trigger erasure, and assert the count across all tables is zero. Run these tests against a real database schema.
At scale, a naive deletion job that issues one
per record becomes a bottleneck. The following patterns keep deletion efficient.
rather than per-row deletes. For large datasets, batch by date range: delete all expired records from a time window in a single statement, then move to the next window.
month. When a partition’s entire date range has expired, drop the partition rather than deleting rows. Partition drops are near-instantaneous even for hundreds of millions of rows.
Queue deletion tasks in a message broker (Kafka, SQS). Workers consume from the queue and execute deletes at a controlled rate, avoiding I/O spikes during peak hours.
The TTL daemon’s query filters on
. Without an index on this column, the query is a full table scan. Add a partial index covering non-null
Object stores containing encrypted blobs can be purged by revoking the KMS key. This is a single API call regardless of how many objects the user’s key encrypted.
Schedule periodic DELETE jobs using pg_cron extension; simple, no extra infra
Set TTL on individual keys at write time; Redis expires them automatically
Move objects to Glacier after N days; delete objects after M days; bucket-level config
Schedule key deletion (7-30 day waiting period); all ciphertext becomes unreadable
Destroy key version; supports customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) across GCP services
Lease-based secrets with automatic expiry; key rotation and revocation API
Track which derived datasets originate from which raw records; critical for derived-data deletion
Model deletion as a durable workflow; handles retries, partial failures, and cross-system coordination
Tag data assets with retention labels; enforce via column-level access controls
Strong; industry standard for cloud storage
All tiers should use AES-256. Use customer-managed keys (CMK) rather than provider-managed keys so you control key lifecycle independently of the storage provider.
Even within retention period, not all users should see all fields. Apply field-level masking in the API layer so that PII fields are redacted for roles that do not need them.

Use an append-only store (S3 Object Lock in WORM mode, or a Postgres table with a trigger that raises an error on UPDATE or DELETE) for deletion audit records. A mutable audit log provides no compliance value.
The deletion job should tolerate being killed mid-run and restarted. Use a transactional delete + audit write pattern so that either both succeed or neither does.

A data breach impact assessment requires knowing what data existed at the time of the breach. Retention records (what was kept, when it was deleted) are as important as the data itself for breach notification obligations.
The TTL daemon and erasure service should run with minimal permissions — only the ability to delete from specific tables and write to the audit log. Do not give them read access to record payloads.
Data retention is a pipeline, not a database field. It requires classification at write time, a policy engine, automated tiering and deletion, and verified audit logging.
at creation time. Retrofitting this metadata later is expensive and error-prone.
Crypto-shredding (revoke the KMS key) is the most scalable deletion mechanism for object stores and is the industry standard for cloud-native retention at scale.
Right-to-erasure is a distinct operation from TTL expiry: it fires immediately on user request and must cover all tables, derived artifacts, and backups, but may exclude records under a legal hold.
Soft deletes do not satisfy GDPR Article 17. Compliance requires the bytes to be gone or the encryption key to be revoked.
Partition-by-time and batch deletes are the primary performance levers for large-scale retention. A single index on
is the minimum required for an efficient TTL daemon.

Audit records are a separate category with their own, often longer, retention requirements. Store them in an append-only medium and never subject them to the same deletion pipeline as operational data.
